summary: Khailo revisits Richard Gabriel's 'Worse is Better' debate, arguing that software only gains value when it's running and touching human hands. He reconciles the tension between shipping quickly and shipping correctly by proposing that both frequent delivery and fault-free code are achievable with discipline, concluding that 'Serving is Better.'
key_points: Software has no value until it is running and serving people
All software is fundamentally a service, even without a network connection
Fault-free software is achievable through discipline, good design, and fixing bugs immediately
The 'Worse is Better' vs 'Right is Better' debate is a false dichotomy
Frequent release and high quality can coexist through disciplined practice
money_quotes: Ultimately what we build is sculpted by human hands, and only when it touches our hands does it form.
ALL software is a service, even when it doesn't have a network connection, all software is there to serve our needs and therefore when it isn't running, it serves nobody.
Our role as professionals and human beings is to build software that serves and prevent software that harms.
Where I conclude is not Worse is Better, or Right is Better, but Serving is Better.
key_insight: The debate between shipping fast and shipping right is a false dichotomy — what matters is serving people, which requires both frequent delivery and disciplined quality.
